message.content: Civil law systems are based primarily on comprehensive, codified statutes enacted by the legislature, with judges applying these codes to resolve disputes but playing a relatively limited role in developing the law, and judicial decisions having persuasive rather than binding force. Common law systems, by contrast, give central importance to judge-made law, with courts creating and refining legal rules through case-by-case adjudication and the doctrine of stare decisis, under which higher courts’ precedents bind lower courts, so that judicial decisions themselves are a primary source of law alongside statutes.
